fig_competitive_positioning_map: Competitive positioning of 12 AI coding agent security vendors on two axes: x-axis = specificity to AI coding agents (0 = generic LLM security, 1.0 = agent-native), y-axis = runtime enforcement level (0 = static scan only, 1.0 = hook-layer real-time enforcement). Prismor (star marker, red) occupies the upper-right quadrant uniquely, combining the highest agent specificity with the deepest runtime enforcement. All competitors cluster in three lower quadrants: generic LLM runtime enforcers (Lakera AI, Palo Alto AIRS, CrowdStrike AIDR), agent-adjacent static tools (Snyk/Invariant, Runlayer, Endor Labs, Pillar Security), and generic static LLM security (Wiz AI-SPM, LLM Guard, Zenity).
fig_market_growth_gen_ai_cybersecurity: Generative AI in cybersecurity market size (in USD billions) from 2025 to 2031, combining a bar chart and an overlaid trend line. Market grows from $8.65B in 2025 to $35.46B in 2031, representing a compound annual growth rate (CAGR) of 26.5%. Each bar is annotated with the absolute market size for that year. Source: MarketsandMarkets.
fig_threat_incident_timeline: Horizontal timeline of 10 documented AI coding agent security incidents spanning March 2025 through April 2026, plotted with colored dot markers by severity: red (Critical) and orange (High). Events alternate above and below the axis to prevent label overlap. The timeline illustrates an accelerating cadence of high-severity incidents, including five Critical events in the first four months of 2026 alone, culminating in the Flowise RCE at CVSS 10.0 and the MCP SDK by-design RCE affecting 150 million downloads.
fig_capability_coverage_heatmap: Table-style heatmap mapping 12 vendors across seven capability dimensions. Binary capabilities (Claude Code Hook, Secrets Cloaking, Post-hoc Sweep, MCP Detection, Air-gap/Local, Policy-as-Code) are colored green (YES), amber (Partial), or red (NO). The OWASP coverage column uses a red-to-green gradient scaled out of 10. The Prismor row (highlighted with a red border) is the only row achieving YES across all six binary capabilities and the highest OWASP score (6.5/10). Data sourced from systematic evaluation of vendor documentation (experimental log Section 8).
fig_prismor_system_architecture: Three-layer block diagram of the Prismor defense-in-depth architecture. The top layer (blue) represents the developer workstation running Claude Code, Cursor, Windsurf, or OpenClaw. The middle layer contains three side-by-side components: CLOAK (orange) performing pre-emptive secret substitution via PreToolUse, PostToolUse, and UserPromptSubmit hooks; WARDEN (red) enforcing 25+ YAML policy rules across 10 threat categories in observe or enforce mode; and SWEEP (purple) scanning agent caches and transcripts with 170+ Gitleaks patterns and AES-256-CBC vault backup. The bottom layer (gray) provides the Immunity Feed of 217 Ed25519-signed advisories and the JSONL plus SQLite audit trail. Arrows indicate data flow from workstation through the defense layers to the intelligence and audit substrate.
fig_secrets_leakage_comparison: Two-panel figure illustrating AI-driven secret leakage risk. Left panel: grouped bar chart comparing the commit-level secret leak rate for AI coding agent (Claude Code) commits at 3.2 percent versus the 1.5 percent human baseline, annotated with the 2.13x multiplier. Right panel: bar chart showing hardcoded secrets on public GitHub for 2024 and 2025, with total secrets growing from approximately 21.4 million to 28.65 million (+34% year-over-year) and AI service credentials growing from approximately 0.70 million to 1.28 million (+81% year-over-year). Source: GitGuardian State of Secrets Sprawl 2026.
